content.assessment: This proposal treats admission-mechanism redesign as an isolated fix within the existing 4/6/8-year architecture. The whole-system reform lens indicates this is precisely the kind of non-structural, single-lever intervention that comparative evidence suggests cannot substitute for coordinated structural change. Coaching-resistant exam redesign addresses one entry point but leaves untouched the primary-school choice sorting that precedes and independently drives segregation, and leaves Roma/regional equity gaps entirely unaddressed since these operate on dimensions (ethnicity, settlement type, early school-leaving) distinct from exam format.
opportunities: Could be combined with T3's anti-sorting catchment rules to jointly address both entry-exam bias and primary-sorting, better approximating the bundled reforms that comparative cases show are necessary
strengths: Uses an existing administrative lever (exam redesign) without requiring new legal architecture, which is procedurally efficient even if substantively partial
Explicitly acknowledges its own limitation regarding geographic access gaps and non-applicant pools, showing awareness that admissions-mechanism fixes alone are insufficient
threats: SES-proxy gaming by advantaged families with political access could hollow out the reform's intended equity effect, consistent with the lens's caution that non-structural quality/equity measures alone rarely achieve durable gains
Coaching investment may simply migrate to new exam formats, reproducing the same SES-driven advantage the reform is meant to eliminate
weaknesses: Does not touch free school choice or primary-school sorting occurring before age 10, which the lens evidence identifies as an independent segregation driver distinct from formal tracking
Fails to address Roma and regional equity gaps that operate through mechanisms (early school-leaving, village/regional disadvantage) unrelated to exam format
As a single isolated lever, it cannot achieve what comparative cases show requires bundling with curricular and choice-regulation reform
